title: How Do I Get My Teacher to Raise My Borderline Grade?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your final grade is an 89.4%. You need a 90% for an A-. You want to ask your teacher to round up. But you don't want to sound entitled or lazy. Here is how to have that conversation professionally — and actually increase your chances of success.

The Wrong Way to Ask

Don't send an email the night before grades are due. Don't say 'I need an A for my scholarship.' Don't beg for extra credit that you could have done weeks ago.

Teachers see this every semester. They are tired of it. It rarely works. It often annoys them. Don't be that student.

The Right Way: Ask Early

If you're worried about your grade, ask weeks before the final. 'I'm currently at an 88%. I'd like to finish with an A-. What can I do between now and the end of the semester to improve my grade?'

This shows initiative. It shows you care about learning, not just the grade. It gives the teacher time to help you. It works much better than last-minute pleading.

What to Say If It's Already the End of the Semester

If you missed the window, here is a professional approach. Send an email or visit during office hours. Say:

'I know grades are due soon. I saw that my final grade is [X]. I was hoping to end with [Y]. I understand if it's not possible, but I wanted to ask if there's anything I can do to improve my grade at this point.'

This is humble. It acknowledges the teacher's constraints. It asks for a solution, not a favor. It's much more likely to get a positive response.

The 'Check My Work' Strategy

Instead of asking for a higher grade, ask the teacher to review a specific assignment. 'I noticed I got a 75 on the research paper. Could you look at it again? I thought I followed the rubric closely.'

If there was a grading error, the teacher might adjust it. If there wasn't, you've at least shown that you care about your work. That might influence the teacher's willingness to round up.

What to Do If the Teacher Says No

Accept it gracefully. 'Thank you for considering my request. I understand.' Don't argue. Don't escalate to the department chair unless there was a clear grading error or unfair treatment.

Arguing won't change the grade. It will only damage your relationship with the teacher. You might need a recommendation letter from them someday. Don't burn that bridge over one grade boundary.

The Truth About Rounding

Many teachers have a policy. They round 89.5% to 90%. They don't round 89.4%. They are consistent. If that's the policy, no amount of asking will change it. Accept the grade you earned.

Some teachers have discretion. They might round up if you were engaged, participated, and showed improvement. They are less likely to round up if you were absent, disengaged, or did the bare minimum.

Your behavior all semester matters more than your last-minute request.

Focus on What You Can Control

If you're early in the semester, you have time. Ask the teacher for help now. Go to office hours. Do the extra credit if it's offered. Study harder for the next exam.

If the semester is over, the grade is likely final. Learn from it. Next semester, start strong. Don't leave your grade to a last-minute prayer.

One borderline grade will not ruin your life. It will not keep you out of college. It will not define you. Let it go. Do better next time.
